expectations.text: Questions target specific gaps in the prompt rather than restating what was already said
expectations.evidence: Identifies key unknowns (sync mechanism, secret protection, config granularity, existing workflow, audience). Questions probe these gaps rather than restating stated requirements.

expectations.text: Questions use concrete scenarios rather than abstract inquiries (e.g. specific conflict situations, not just 'how do you handle conflicts')
expectations.evidence: Most questions are abstract: Q1 'What is your current dotfile workflow, and what specifically is breaking or painful about it?' Q2 'What is the sync/transport mechanism you have in mind?' Q8 'What is your preferred language/runtime for the CLI?' These are valid but generic. Only Q4 approaches a concrete scenario: 'When there is a conflict -- say you changed .zshrc on both machines before syncing -- what should happen?' Compared to the with-skill version which paints vivid scenarios, this is significantly more abstract.

expectations.text: Strategy explicitly plans to use AskUserQuestion one at a time
expectations.evidence: No mention of AskUserQuestion. Questions are presented as a numbered list without specifying the interaction mechanism.

expectations.text: At least one question probes an intersection or tension between stated requirements (e.g. private/public vs work/personal)
expectations.evidence: No question explicitly intersects requirements. Q3 asks about private/public and Q7 asks about audience, but neither probes how these dimensions interact with each other or create tensions.

expectations.text: Strategy describes adapting follow-up questions based on user answers
expectations.evidence: The approach summary mentions 'an adaptive follow-up plan based on possible answers (e.g., git-backed leads to repo structure questions; value-level secrets leads to encryption tooling questions).'
